reasoning: Iteration 1 regressed, but the two critical correctness bugs (inverted l_shipdate filter logic on line 291 and lossy GROUP BY key encoding on line 306) must be fixed before any performance work. With only 1 iteration remaining, prioritize fixing both correctness issues and implementing the single highest-impact optimization: thread-based parallelism on the 60-core system. Current single-threaded execution wastes 98.4% of hardware resources and is the dominant bottleneck. Parallelism can deliver 30-64x wall-clock speedup and is proven low-risk.
focus_areas: Fix line 291: invert l_shipdate filter logic to correctly keep rows where l_shipdate > threshold (replace 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' with proper semantics)
Fix line 306: expand 64-bit GROUP BY key encoding to include all three columns: l_orderkey (32-bit), o_orderdate (16-bit), o_shippriority (16-bit)

Implement morsel-driven thread parallelism across 64 cores: partition lineitem into ~64 morsels (~1M rows each), launch worker threads for parallel scan/filter/aggregate, merge thread-local aggregation tables
Ensure thread safety: use thread-local hash tables with final global merge to avoid lock contention and synchronization overhead
strategy_notes: This is the final iteration (2/3). MUST fix correctness first: both semantic bugs block valid results. Then apply the highest-impact optimization proven to work: thread parallelism addresses the 98.4% CPU underutilization and 607K page fault rate. Use morsel-driven approach from knowledge base (parallelism/thread-parallelism.md). After correctness is restored and parallelism is in place, current GenDB time (386ms baseline) should drop significantly toward DuckDB's 77-112ms range (expected 50-100ms with parallelism on columnar binary storage). Skip lower-priority optimizations (zone maps, compact hash tables, SIMD, join reordering) to maintain focus on correctness + primary bottleneck elimination.
